coredata.dc:title: Eco-friendly synthesis of PbTiO3 nanoparticles and PbTiO3/carbon quantum dots binary nano-hybrids for enhanced photocatalytic performance under visible light
coredata.dc:creator: Kooshki, Hamid
Sobhani-Nasab, Ali
Eghbali-Arani, Mohammad
Ahmadi, Farhad
Ameri, Vahid
Rahimi-Nasrabadi, Mehdi
coredata.dc:description: PbTiO3 nanoparticles, carbon quantum dots CQDs and their composite (i.e. PbTiO3/CQDs) were prepared and studied using XRD, FT-IR, VSM, TEM, BET, PL, FESEM, UV–Vis, and EDX. Designing highly-effective PbTiO3/CQDs binary nano-hybrids include several fascinating properties of visible light, biocompatibility, eco-friendly response and good durability. Various composites containing different amounts of CQDs were prepared, and the effects of catalysts on enhancing the photocatalytic activity of PbTiO3 nanoparticles in the resulting binary nano-hybrid have been investigated. Test experiments indicated that PbTiO3 nanoparticles could remove only 53% of Rh B, while PbTiO3/5%CQDs binary nano-hybrids reached a removal maximum of 100%. Further the removal of different dyes using the PbTiO3/CQDs binary nano-hybrids has been evaluated and the removal mechanism has also been discussed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PbTiO3
PbTiO3/CQDs
Visible light
Binary nano-hybrids
Eco-friendly method

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the PbTiO3/CQDs binary nano-hybrid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern of (a) carbon dots (b) PbTiO3 nanoparticles obtained in the presence of (b) Tween 20 (c) Tween 80 (d) Ethyl cellulose and (e) PbTiO3/5% CQDs binary nano-hybrids.
EDS pattern of PbTiO3/5% CQDs binary nano-hybrids (Sample No. 6).
SEM images of PbTiO3 nanoparticles obtained in the presence of (a) Tween 20 (b) Tween 80 (c) Ethyl cellulose.
TEM image of (a) carbon dots (b) PbTiO3 nanoparticles obtained in the presence of ethyl cellulose (sample No. 3) (c) PbTiO3/5% CQDs binary nano-hybrids (sample No. 5).
FT-IR spectra of (a) PbTiO3 nanoparticles (sample No. 3) and (b) PbTiO3/5% CQDs binary nano-hybrids (sample No. 5).
BET pattern of (a,b) PbTiO3 nanoparticles (sample No. 3) and (c,d) PbTiO3/5% CQDs binary nano-hybrids (sample No. 5).
VSM curve of PbTiO3/5% CQDs binary nano-hybrids (Sample No 6).
Room temperature PL spectra of carbon dots.

UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrum (a) PbTiO3 nanoparticles and PbTiO3/5% CQDs binary nano-hybrids and (b) Tauc plot pattern PbTiO3 nanoparticles and PbTiO3/5% CQDs binary nano-hybrids (sample No. 3 and 5).

(a) The photocatalytic behavior of PbTiO3 nanoparticles (sample No. 1–3), (b) The photocatalytic behavior of PbTiO3 nanoparticles, PbTiO3/2% CQDs, PbTiO3/5% CQDs and PbTiO3/10% CQDs binary nano-hybrids (sample No. 4–6), (c) Absorption spectra the RhB solution in the presence of PbTiO3/5% CQDs.

(a) the photocatalytic behavior of PbTiO3/5% CQDs binary nano-hybrids (sample No. 5) on decomposition of various contaminants (cationic and anionic types), and (b) effects of the reuse of the PbTiO3/5% CQDs binary nano-hybrids on the degradation efficiency of RhB (sample No. 5).
Schematic illustration of charge transfer in a coupled semiconductor system (PbTiO3/5% CQDs binary nano-hybrids).
The preparation conditions of PbTiO3 nanoparticles, PbTiO3/CQDs binary nano-hybrids.
The adsorption/desorption parameter of the PbTiO3 nanoparticles, PbTiO3/CQDs binary nano-hybrids.
The optical properties of the PbTiO3 nanoparticles, PbTiO3/CQDs binary nano-hybrids.

row.entry: Rate constant of Rh B degradation (h−1)

head.ce:title: Eco-friendly synthesis of PbTiO3 nanoparticles and PbTiO3/carbon quantum dots binary nano-hybrids for enhanced photocatalytic performance under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanobiotechnology Research Center, Baqiyatallah University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Young Researchers and Elites Club, Arak Branch, Islamic Azad University, Arak, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, University of Kashan, Kashan, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Medicinal Chemistry, School of Pharmacy-International Campus, Iran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Faculty of Science, University of Hormozgan, Bandar-Abbas, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: f
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Pharmacy, Baqiyatallah University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Nanobiotechnology Research Center, Baqiyatallah University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: PbTiO3 nanoparticles, carbon quantum dots CQDs and their composite (i.e. PbTiO3/CQDs) were prepared and studied using XRD, FT-IR, VSM, TEM, BET, PL, FESEM, UV–Vis, and EDX. Designing highly-effective PbTiO3/CQDs binary nano-hybrids include several fascinating properties of visible light, biocompatibility, eco-friendly response and good durability. Various composites containing different amounts of CQDs were prepared, and the effects of catalysts on enhancing the photocatalytic activity of PbTiO3 nanoparticles in the resulting binary nano-hybrid have been investigated. Test experiments indicated that PbTiO3 nanoparticles could remove only 53% of Rh B, while PbTiO3/5%CQDs binary nano-hybrids reached a removal maximum of 100%. Further the removal of different dyes using the PbTiO3/CQDs binary nano-hybrids has been evaluated and the removal mechanism has also been discussed.

ce:section.ce:para: A major source of water pollution, as a serious environmental issue, is synthetic dyes present in wastewaters [1–4]. These compounds have low biodegradability and are chemically stable in aqueous media, which increases the severity of the environmental problems caused by them [5]. Various efforts have been focused on eliminating organic dyes from wastewaters, among which the application of photocatalysts as effective yet low-cost tools to this end offers great potentials. Various research studies have been made on the wide band gaps of semiconducting materials like TiO2, ZnO, and ZnS recently. These semiconducting materials can be only excited under UV radiation [6–10]. Titanates are common semiconductors with favorable electrical and optical properties, reasonable costs, high biological and environmental safety, and good photocatalytic degradation abilities for organic species under UV light [11,12]. Yet the compounds have a wide band gap (∼3.2 eV) and cannot be excited under visible light (i.e. 43% of solar spectrum) limiting their photocatalytic applications. To enhance catalytic effects of these compounds under visible light heterojunction of titanate-based semiconductors has been used as a method to tune the band potentials of the resulting materials so as to reach desired photocatalytic activities.

On the other hand carbon-based materials like carbon nanofibers, nanotubes and nanodots, and graphene have recently been found as potential materials for preparing composites in different application due to their excellent conductivity and chemical characteristics [13–17]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been found to offer better photophysical and chemical properties as opposed to the other carbon materials and has hence been receiving increasing attention [18]. Although decorating noble metal nanoparticles on binary composites is a common approach, the increasing costs of such approaches, causes serious limitations in the way of practically using heterojunction [19]. Fortunately, the low costs of CQDs, as a favorable class of nanostructured carbon-based materials with good dissolubility in water and excellent electron transfer properties changes them to good candidates for use as a multifunctional component in preparing photocatalysts with a wider absorption spectrum [20,21].

This study was focused on preparing a binary nano-hybrid of PbTiO3 nanoparticles and PbTiO3/CQDs through an eco-friendly method based on the application of Tween 20, Tween 80 and Ethyl cellulose for the preparation of PbTiO3 nanoparticles for the first time. PbTiO3/2%CQDs, PbTiO3/5%CQDs, and PbTiO3/10%CQDs binary nano-hybrids were prepared and the crystalline phases, structure, morphology, specific surface area and optical properties of the samples were studied through X-ray diffraction, scanning electron microscopy, Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET), and UV–visible absorption. Then photocatalytic experiments were performed using the different samples under visible light. Also comparative studies were performed using pristine PbTiO3. The results indicated a considerable enhancement in the photocatalytic reaction in the case of the PbTiO3/5%CQDs binary nano-hybrids. The effect of incorporating CQDs in the photocatalysts was also studied and the mechanism of the photocatalytic activity of the binary nano-hybrids was discussed in detail.

All chemicals including Pb(NO3)2·4H2O, tetra-n-butyl titanate, sodium citrate, NH4HCO3, Tween 20, Tween 80 and ethyl cellulose were from Merck Co. and were not subjected to any further treatments. The XRD results were obtained using a Philips-X’pertpro instrument with an Ni-filtered Cu Ka radiation. The FT-IR spectra were acquired using a Nicolet Magna- 550 spectrometer and KBr pellets. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were recorded using a LEO-1455VP instrument energy dispersive X-ray spectroscope (EDX) at a 20 kV acceleration voltage. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a Philips EM208 TEM at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Diffuse reflectance UV–visible spectrums (DRS) were recorded on a Jasco V-670 UV–Vis instrument. The magnetic measurement of sample was carried out in a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) (Meghnatis Daghigh Kavir Co.; Kashan Kavir; Iran) at room temperature.

CQDs were prepared using sodium citrate through a simple one-step hydrothermal approach. The method typically involved mixing 0.2 g of sodium citrate, 1.5 g of NH4HCO3 and 10 mL of water in a sealed Teflon coated stainless steel autoclave. The system was next placed in an oven for the hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 240 min. The resulting dark brown precipitate was next separated through centrifugation at 12000 rpm for 15 min.

The starting materials used were Pb(NO3)2·6H2O, Tetra-n-butyl Titanate, and various capping agents (Tween 20, Tween 80 and Ethyl cellulose), and the optimal conditions were determined through different experiments. The conditions and results are presented in Table 1 . The typical method involved dissolving 1 mmol of Pb(NO3)2·6H2O in 50 mL of distilled water, followed by adding aqueous solutions containing 3 mmol of the capping agent to these solutions. Then stoichiometric quantities of tetraethyl orthotitanate (Ti4+) were added to the solution to reach the desired molar ratios of Pb2+ to Ti4+ as presented in Table 1. The resulting solutions were vigorously stirred at 60 °C for half an hour and the obtained cream precipitate was next collected, dried at 100 °C and finally was calcined at 600 °C for 120 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the PbTiO3/CQDs binary nano-hybrid
ce:section.ce:para: The PbTiO3/CQDs binary nano-hybrids, were prepared through dispersing desired quantities of the nanostructures PbTiO3 in 30 mL of the ethanol under sonication at 240 W for 15 min. Then, up to a %2, %5 and 10% Wt mixture of the CQDs were added to the dispersions under sonication at 240 W for 15 min. Finally, the product was dried at 90 °C for 2 h, which its details are presented in Table 1.

The effect of the presence of the binary nano-hybrids on the concentration of different dyes was studied using a quartz photocatalytic reactor and 5 × 10−5 M of solutions of the dyes further containing 0.05 g of the binary nano-hybrids. Before transferring the mixtures to the photoreactor, the nanostructure with solution stirred in the dark for 30 min to an adsorption/ desorption equilibrium between the dye and the photocatalyst. The sample vessel in the photoreactor was placed 20 cm away from the 400 W Osram lamp (6 5 0 〉 λ > 400 nm). Both before and at 10 min intervals during the reaction samples of the reaction mixture were taken and studied through UV–Vis spectrometery after centrifugation.

The XRD pattern of synthesized CQDs (Fig. 1 a) illustrates the poor crystalline properties of the product. The broad peak centered at 2ϴ = 24 reflects a spacing of 3.4 Å which exceeds that of bulk graphite (3.3 Å). The data further indicate the purity of the CQDs.

The XRD results for PbTiO3 nanostructures prepared using Tween 20, Tween 80 and ethyl cellulose are presented in Fig. 1b–d. In the case of bare PbTiO3 a series of diffraction peaks can be observed at 26.32° ((1 1 2) line), 32.18° ((0 0 2) line), 43.18° ((2 0 0) line), and 54.63° ((2 2 2) line), which very well compatible with the data from standard JCPDS file of PbTiO3 tetragonal phase (space group I41/a, JCPDS No. 85-0588).

The XRD patterns obtained for PbTiO3/2%CQDs, PbTiO3/5%CQDs and PbTiO3/10%CQDs binary nano-hybrids are presented in Fig. 1a–j, respectively. Based on these results, all samples were pure and composed of two phases, i.e. tetragonal PbTiO3 and CQDs crystal phase (JCPDS 85-0588; space group: I41) and tetragonal PbTiO3 and CQDs crystal phase (JCPDS 85-0888; space group: I41/a). Considering Fig. 1 and Scherrer's equation, the crystallite diameter (Dc) of products can be obtained as following [22]:
(1)
D c
= K λ / β cos θ ;

In this equation β represents the breadth of a diffraction line at its half intensity maxima, K is a shape factor (approximately = 0.9), and λ is the X-ray wavelength used. The domain sizes of evaluated crystalline have been found out to be 16/2, 13/4 and 11 nm, for different PbTiO3 (Fig. 1b and c) respectively and PbTiO3/CQDs binary nano-hybrids (12/3) were determined.
EDS results proved the PbTiO3/5%CQDs binary nano-hybrids to be pure. For instance, Fig. 2 (sample No. 1) indicates that the PbTiO3/5%CQDs binary nano-hybrid was merely composed of Pb, Ti, C and O.

We used natural and ploymeric capping agent for the synthesis of nanoparticles [23–29]. These capping agents can bond with the surface of PbTiO3 nanoparticles, since they are polymeric and can have a large number of hydroxyl groups, and consequently can stop the growth of nanoparticles. As Fig. 3 a–c shows nanoparticles were prepared in the presence of capping agents of Tween 80, Tween 20, and ethyl cellulose. The SEM images display that synthesized PbTiO3 nanoparticles in the presence of all capping agents had spherical morphology, whereas in the presence of capping agent ethyl cellulose nanoparticles had uniform distribution.

The prepared carbon dots through sodium citrate have been shown in Fig. 4 a, which have the size of 3–5 nm. The image of prepared nanoparticles in the presence of ethyl cellulose demonstrated in Fig. 4b represents that these nanoparticles size between 30 and 50 nm.

According to some analyses such as SEM, XRD, and TEM we found that nanoparticles prepared in the presence of ethyl cellulose had smaller and good distribution size, as a result, we utlized them as our samples to produce nanocomposites.
In addition, Fig. 4c shows PbTiO3/CQDs binary nano-hybrids which carbon dots have been load on the surface of PbTiO3 nanopartcles.

For evaluating the quality and chemical composition of the PbTiO3 and PbTiO3/5% CQDs binary nano-hybrids, FTIR analysis was performed in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 and the results are given in Fig. 5 a and b. The bond at 3438 cm−1 reflects the presence of OH groups [30–32], and those at 572 and 717 cm−1 correspond to the stretching of TiO bond and bending of OTiO bonds. This confirms the formation of PbTiO3. In the PbTiO3/5% CQDs samples (the red plot) the peaks related to CQDs can be further observed. The peaks at around 1631 cm−1 and 1403 cm−1 correspond to the CO, and CC bonds respectively [33].

The result of evaluating the magnetic property of PbTiO3/5% CQDs through VSM at 300 K is presented in Fig. 7 , indicating that the sample has ferromagnetic properties and a magnetization value of around 0.042 emu/g.

The optical characteristics of the CQDs were evaluated through photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy at room temperature at 550 nm. The spectrum obtained for CQDs contains a blue 1 For interpretation of color in Fig. 8, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.
1 maximum emission at about 521 nm as shown in Fig. 8
[34].

The UV–visible absorption spectra of the PbTiO3 and PbTiO3/5% COD are shown in Fig. 9 . PbTiO3 and PbTiO3/5% COD have a strong absorption maximum 345 and 427 nm, respectively. The absorption maximum of the sample PbTiO3/5% COD is shifted to higher wavelengths. This red shift absorption in the visible region, which implies the PbTiO3/5% CODs composite may give rise to more photo-generated charge than pure PbTiO3 under visible light. The band gap energy of the samples are measured by the extrapolation of the linear portion of the graph between the modified Kubelka-Munk function [F(R)hν]2 versus photon energy) hν) [35], as shown in Fig. 9b. Moreover, the band-gap energy for pure PbTiO3 and PbTiO3/5% COD are nearly 2.4, and 2.8 eV, respectively. Comparing with the pure PbTiO3 sample, the band gap of the PbTiO3/5% COD binary nano-hybrids reduces which is beneficial for generation of more photogenerated charge carriers under visible-light irradiation. The band gaps of the samples are shown in Table 2.

Fig. 10 a shows the effects of the application of different capping agents (e.g. Tween 20, Tween 80 and ethyl cellulose) on the photocatalytic efficiency of PbTiO3 nanoparticles (samples 1–3). Evidently the binary nano-hybrids prepared using ethyl cellulose (sample No. 3) which had a degradation rate of 53% were concluded as the best photocatalyst.

The results of a blank test, in the absence of photocatalysts, is illustrated in Fig. 10a, which indicated the stability of Rh B under visible light and the results indicated a 5% removal under visible light.

To verify the absorbent effect of dye, we did a test in the presence of catalyst (Only sample 3) without light and it was proved that up to 3 percent of dyes were removed during first half an hour of test. Afterwards, the figure was fixed. As a result, for all samples, we put catalysts and dye solution in dark place. Also, for investigation of how absorbent of dye can influence photocatalyst function, we performed another test in without dark place (Without absorbent) which showed a reduction up to 14℅ in photocatalyst destruction.

Fig. 10b shows that PbTiO3 nanoparticles (sample No. 3) could remove the pollutant only to a maximum of 52% and this value reached 99% using PbTiO3/5% CQDs (sample No. 5). The photocatalytic activities of bare PbTiO3, and PbTiO3/5%CQDs under visible light were also evaluated. Fig. 10b illustrates a time-dependent profiles of the degradation of Rh B under these conditions. The linear plots of ln(Ct/C0)=kappt versus time (C0 and C being the Rh B concentration at the beginning and at any instant t during the reaction, respectively) indicate that the kinetics of the photocatalytic decomposition reaction follow the pseudo-first-order Langmuir-Hinshelwood model. Table 3 contains the apparent first-order rate constants (k) and clearly shows that bare PbTiO3/5% CQDs binary nano-hybrids sample only operated under visible light (k = 0.0934 h−1) while PbTiO3 nanoparticles have very low activity under visible light (Table 3)

This indicated that the incorporation of CQDs has a substantial effect on the photocatalytic activity of the PbTiO3 nanoparticles. It is noteworthy that PbTiO3 can contribute to a red shift in the binary nano-hybrid reducing the band gap and increasing the adsorption in visible light. Further the incorporation of CQDs can lead to the appropriate distribution of PbTiO3 nanoparticles, facilitate the absorption of Rh B and its transfer to the active sites on PbTiO3 nanoparticles, and also charge carrier separation, hence enhancing the removal efficiency of PbTiO3 nanoparticles [36].
Fig. 10(c) shows the absorption spectra displayed the degradation of Rh B with PbTiO3/5%CQDs binary nano-hybrids under visible-light irradiation.
Recently nanoparticles and nanocomposites are frequently being used as catalysts [37–47].

The PbTiO3/5%CQDs binary nano-hybrids and PbTiO3 nanoparticles were also tested for eliminating 4 different dyes, i.e. rhodamine B (Rh B), methyl violet (MV), phenol red (Ph R) and methyl orange (MO) under visible irradiation. The results (Fig. 11 a) clearly indicate that the highest photodecomposition activity was still can be seen in the case of Rh B. The respective photodecomposition efficiencies binary nano-hybrids for Rh B, MV, PhR and MO were 98%, 91%, 71%, and 65% and for PbTiO3 nanoparticles 52%, 42%, 38%, and 33%.

The stability of the PbTiO3/5%CQDs binary nano-hybrids was also evaluated through the repeated application of the binary nano-hybrids and according to the results (Fig. 11b), the PbTiO3/5%CQDs binary nano-hybrids (sample No. 5) underwent a very small activity loss after 5 consecutive reactions and its degradation efficiency reached 90% after 5 cycles. These results clearly indicate PbTiO3/5%CQDs as a useful and efficient photocatalyst for use in visible light.

Based on the analyses and experimental results, a tentative mechanism was proposed for the photocatalytic reaction of PbTiO3/5%CQDs binary nano-hybrids (Scheme 1 ). According to this mechanism initially PbTiO3 nanoparticles absorbs visible light, and transfers electrons from the valence band to conduction band (CB). This leaves h+ in the valence band (VB). Usually, the photogenerated electron/hole pairs quickly recombine in the absence of the co-catalysts lowering the photocatalystic activity (as in the case of pure PbTiO3 nanoparticles).

Further, CQDs have a lower unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) level than PbTiO3, when CQDs were anchored on the surface of PbTiO3, the photogenerated electrons from the CB of PbTiO3 can easily transfer to CQDs through photo-induced charge transfer process and the h+ leaves in the valence band of PbTiO3 for CQDs can act as both electron acceptors and donors [48], thus effectively improves charge separation by the rapid photo-induced charge transfer.
Malachite green methyl violet

Next the photo-electrons on the CQDs can also combine with the oxygen present in the aqueous solution. As a result O2
−, which is a strong oxidizing agent, is formed and can decompose Rh B. Given that the VBs of PbTiO3 nanoparticles (1.540 eV vs. NHE) are below the standard redox potential of E (OH
/OH−) (1.99 eV vs. NHE) [49], the holes present in PbTiO3 nanoparticles cannot immediately oxidize OH− or H2O to OH
radicals. Hence the main source of the OH
radicals is the reaction of O2
− with photogenerated electrons and the water molecules and the partial h+ in the valence bands of PbTiO3 nanoparticles can directly oxidize the pollutants.

PbTiO3 nanoparticles were prepared using various natural polymers (i.e. Tween 20, Tween 80 and ethyl cellulose) and the effects of the polymers as green capping agents on the shape and size of the final products were evaluated, for the first time. Also PbTiO3/CQD binary nano-hybrids were successfully prepared via a facile method. PbTiO3/5%CQDs binary nano-hybrids were found to exhibits high-efficiency photocatalytic effects toward Rh B under visible light, which was attributed to the enhanced absorption of the visible light, excellent charge separation, and they synergy of the photogenerated change transfer properties of CQDs and semiconductor particles in the binary nano-hybrids. The photocatalytic efficiency of the samples were evaluated and the highest and lowest degradation efficiencies were 99% and 60.3% for Rh B and MO. The present work provides an insight on the design of enhanced carbon-based photocatalysts for use in visible region.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wish to thank the National Institute for Medical Research Development (NIMAD) for their support (Grant No. 958654).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2018.10.057.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
